card_title: 3 ways CNY actually reaches your supplier

videos.video_title: The 3 paths your CNY takes to China (most operators only know one)

variants.vo: We watch operators send the same supplier three wires in a row and get three different speeds. First one takes five days. Second one takes two. Third one lands the same day. They think the bank is being random. It isn't. There are three different roads your money can travel to reach a Chinese supplier. Road one is the old international banking network. Your money gets passed from bank to bank to bank before it arrives. Three to five days. Road two is China's own payment system. Faster, one to two days. Road three is a direct connection to a Chinese bank that handles the local leg. Same day. Your bank picks road one by default and never tells you the other two exist. Check out WorldFirst. We use the fast one.

Quick maths on getting Chinese currency to a Chinese supplier. There are three ways your money can actually travel. Way one. The default international bank route. Your money hops through three or four other banks before it arrives. Three to five days. Each bank along the way takes a fee, fifteen to fifty dollars. Way two. China's own clearing system. One to two days. No middle banks. Way three. A direct link to a Chinese bank that handles the local leg same day. On a fifty thousand dollar wire that's the difference between forty nine thousand landing in five days, or forty nine thousand eight hundred landing today. Your bank picks the slowest one by default. Search WorldFirst. We don't.

Your bank told you a wire to China takes five days. That's not a fact. That's a routing choice. There are three different ways your money can travel to reach a Chinese supplier and your bank put you on the slowest one. The old international bank network was built in the seventies to pass messages between banks. It was never built to move money fast. China has its own system that processes twenty four trillion dollars a year. Your bank pretends it doesn't exist. There's also a direct link to a Chinese bank that lands money same day. Your bank pretends that one doesn't exist either. The slow wire isn't physics. It's a choice. Look up WorldFirst. We use the fast road.

card_title: Your supplier asked you to pay the $200 they didn't receive

videos.video_title: Your supplier just asked for the $200 they did not get

variants.vo: We see this happen on roughly one in ten wires to Chinese suppliers. Operator sends fifty thousand dollars. Next morning a message arrives. We only got forty nine thousand eight hundred and thirty five. Please top up. The bank app says sent. Successful. Done. So who's lying. Neither of them. Here's what really happens. The wire travels through three other banks before it reaches your supplier. Each one is allowed to take its own cut, fifteen to fifty dollars, straight from the amount, before passing it on. Your bank doesn't warn you. Your supplier just sees a smaller number arrive. The missing money isn't a scam. It's three middle banks each taking their slice. Check out WorldFirst. We send the full amount, no middle banks.

Quick maths on why your supplier got less than you sent. A wire to China passes through one to three middle banks on its way. Each one takes fifteen to fifty dollars before passing the rest on. So on a fifty thousand dollar wire, that's forty five to a hundred and fifty dollars gone before your supplier sees a cent. The default setting on your wire says supplier eats those fees. You can change the setting so you pay them yourself, but that costs you another thirty to seventy five dollars upfront, and your bank will quietly mark up the rate to make it back. Or you can use a system with no middle banks at all. Look up WorldFirst. Full amount, every time.

Everyone treats the missing two hundred dollars on a China wire like a mystery. It's not a mystery. It's how the system was built. The international bank network isn't actually a payment system. It's a messaging system from 1973 that lets banks send instructions to each other. The real money moves through middle banks who all get paid for the favour. Each one quietly takes fifteen to fifty dollars from your wire. Your bank wrote a code on your transfer that says the supplier pays those fees, not you. You aren't being scammed. You're being routed through fifty year old infrastructure that was designed to charge per stop. WorldFirst sends money to China direct, no middle banks. Check us out.

videos.video_title: Why your wire arrived 3 days late and short $150

variants.vo: We see the same pattern almost every week. Day one, operator wires thirty thousand to a factory. Day two, supplier is quiet. Day three, still quiet. Day four, the bank is reviewing it. Day five, finally released. Short a hundred and fifty dollars. Production was supposed to start Monday. It's Friday. Two things ate that week. The wire passed through two middle banks, each taking a day and forty dollars. And the supplier's name on the wire didn't exactly match what's on their Chinese business registration. They wrote ABC Trading. The registered name is ABC Trading Co Limited. Chinese banks check that match. Anything off pauses the wire for review. Check out WorldFirst. We verify the supplier's exact registered name before the wire leaves.

Quick maths on a delayed China wire. Two things slow it down. One. Middle banks. A wire to China usually passes through one to three of them. Each one takes a business day to process. Each one takes fifteen to fifty dollars. Two. Name mismatch. Chinese banks check the supplier's name on the wire against their official business registration. If you wrote ABC Trading but the registered name is ABC Trading Co Limited, the wire pauses for review. Three to ten days typical. Five to ten percent of wires to China get held up this way. Two minutes spent matching the exact registered name saves a week. Search WorldFirst. We catch the mismatch before the wire ever leaves.

Your bank told you the wire would clear in two days. It's day five. Your supplier is still chasing. Your bank says it left their system on day one, so this isn't their problem. Here's what they didn't say. Your money is sitting at the second middle bank in the chain, stuck in a review queue, because the supplier's name on the wire didn't exactly match the Chinese business registration. Missing Co Limited. Or English letters instead of Chinese characters. One small gap and the wire stalls for a week. Banks treat this as normal. We treat it as a fixable problem. WorldFirst checks the exact registered supplier name before the wire leaves. Look us up.

card_title: Why paying your supplier in CNY actually wins

videos.video_title: USD feels safer. It costs you 2% more.

variants.vo: We watch operators pay Chinese suppliers in US dollars for years because it feels safer. Then someone runs the numbers. On a twenty thousand dollar order they're losing roughly four hundred dollars per wire. Not in fees. In the exchange rate. Their bank converts local money to US dollars and pockets one percent in the rate. The supplier's Chinese bank then converts US dollars to Chinese currency on the other end and pockets another half percent. Two conversions, both invisible, both paid by the operator. When they switch to paying directly in Chinese currency, one conversion, supplier gets full amount same day. The safety was an illusion costing two percent on every order. Check out WorldFirst. One conversion, no double tax.

Quick maths on paying your Chinese supplier in US dollars versus Chinese currency. US dollar route. Two conversions stack up. Your bank converts your local money to US dollars, half a percent to one and a half percent gets baked into the exchange rate. Supplier's bank then converts US dollars to Chinese currency on arrival, another point three to point eight percent. Combined hidden cost is point eight to two point three percent on top of the invoice. On a twenty thousand dollar order that's a hundred and sixty to four hundred and sixty dollars before anything ships. Chinese currency direct route. One conversion, point three to point five percent. Same payment. Same destination bank. Half the cost. Search WorldFirst.

Everyone tells you to pay in US dollars because it's safe. Safe for who. Your US dollar wire to a Chinese factory hits two conversions. Your bank takes one percent turning your local cash into US dollars. The supplier's bank takes another half percent turning that into Chinese currency. Both costs are invisible because they're hidden in the exchange rate, not the fee line. Meanwhile the supplier is sitting on US dollars they can't actually spend, waiting seven days for clearance, paying their own bank to flip the money. Safe is a marketing word. The cost is real. Pay in Chinese currency direct and the whole stack disappears. WorldFirst is built for this. Check us out.

They pad the US dollar price by three to five percent to protect themselves. Most operators have been paying that markup for years without knowing. Ask your supplier for their Chinese currency price on the next quote. Watch what comes back. Often three percent lower for the same factory, same order, just a different currency on the invoice. Look up WorldFirst. We send Chinese currency direct.

Working capital frozen. Two, their bank takes a fee to convert US dollars into Chinese currency, point three to point eight percent. Three, currency risk if the exchange rate moves between the day they invoiced you and the day the money arrives. On a twenty thousand dollar order that's sixty to a hundred and sixty dollars in fees, plus a week of frozen cashflow, plus exchange rate exposure. So they pad the next invoice by two to five percent to cover it. You've been paying that markup invisibly for years. Search WorldFirst. Pay in Chinese currency direct. Watch the next quote drop.

Your supplier doesn't actually want your US dollars. They just don't say it because they're polite. So they don't refuse it. They just quietly price it in. The US dollar invoice you got is three to five percent higher than the Chinese currency one would be. That's not greed. That's defence. You've been paying for your supplier's exchange rate risk and calling it the invoice price. Ask for the Chinese currency quote. WorldFirst handles this. Check us out.

card_title: The 3-letter code that decides if your wire is same-day

videos.video_title: The 3-letter code on every China wire (most operators never see it)

variants.vo: Most operators don't know this exists. There's a tiny three letter code on every wire that decides who pays the middle bank fees along the way. Three options. One means you pay them all upfront, supplier gets the full amount. Another means the fees get taken out of the wire as it travels, supplier gets less. The third means the supplier pays everything, they get even less. The default setting at almost every bank is the middle one. The one where your supplier eats the deductions. Banks don't ask you which option you want. They just pick. You can ask them to switch it, or you can use a system that has no middle banks at all. WorldFirst was built for this. Look us up.

Quick maths on the three letter code on your China wire. Three options. Option one, you pay all the middle bank fees upfront, your supplier gets the full amount. Costs you thirty to seventy five extra dollars. Option two, the fees get deducted from the wire as it travels. Supplier gets less. On a typical wire to China with two middle banks, that's forty five to a hundred and fifty dollars off the top. Option three, the supplier pays everything including the upfront fees. They get even less. Your bank's default is option two. They picked it for you without asking. Search WorldFirst. We use a system with no middle banks. Different problem, different setup.

Your bank picked a three letter code for you on every wire to China and never asked. The code decides who pays the middle bank fees along the way. They picked the option where your supplier pays them. On a typical wire that means two or three middle banks each take thirty dollars off the top. Your supplier gets less. They flag the shortfall. You top them up. The bank picked that option because it costs them nothing and they don't have to explain it. You could ask them to switch it and pay the fees yourself. Or you could ask why anyone is still using a system from the seventies that has a code for who eats the middleman tax. WorldFirst skips it entirely. Check us out.

card_title: Supplier said wire didn't arrive. Bank says it did.

variants.vo: We see this every week. Day three after the wire. Bank app says sent. Supplier says nothing has arrived. Operator is refreshing both like a maniac. Bank says the money left their system on day one, so it isn't their problem any more. Supplier says nothing visible in their account. Then someone digs and finds the wire sitting at a middle bank in Hong Kong on a compliance hold. Nobody told the operator. Nobody told their bank. The wire wasn't lost. It was sitting in a queue nobody could see. That's the problem with the old international bank network. Three or four other banks handle your money along the way, each one runs its own checks, none of them update you. Check out WorldFirst. Direct route, no invisible queues, same day to Chinese banks eighty percent of the time.

Quick maths on where your wire actually sits when both your bank and your supplier are right. Five places it can get stuck. One, your bank. Half a day. Two, the first middle bank. Can hold the wire up to two days for compliance checks. Three, the second middle bank. Another one to two days. Four, a currency conversion window. Up to two working days more. Five, your supplier's bank. Plus any local holidays in China. Total possible delay, five to ten business days. None of it visible to you. None of it visible to your bank. The only fix is no middle banks at all. Look up WorldFirst. Direct route to Chinese banks. Same day landing eighty percent of the time.

Your bank says the wire was sent. Your supplier says they got nothing. Everyone's telling the truth and the system is what's broken. When a bank says sent, all they mean is the wire left their building. They have no idea where it is now. When your supplier says nothing, they mean their account is empty. The space in between is two or three other banks running compliance checks, doing currency conversions, batching transactions, and not telling anybody what they're doing. The old international bank network was built so banks could send messages to each other. It was never built so you could track your money. WorldFirst uses a direct route. No middle banks. No invisible middle. Search us.

card_title: Why your CNY payment sometimes arrives in USD

videos.video_title: 3 places where your CNY can become USD

variants.vo: We see this happen on roughly one in five wires marked as Chinese currency. The wire form says Chinese currency. The amount is in Chinese currency. Three days later the supplier messages. Why did this arrive as US dollars. I had to convert it again. Lost two hundred dollars. The operator stares at their bank app like, but I sent Chinese currency. What actually happened. The Chinese currency turned into US dollars at three different points before reaching the supplier. The sending bank flipped it to US dollars because they only hold US dollars in their treasury. The middle bank only handled US dollars. The receiving bank had to convert US dollars back into Chinese currency on the supplier's end. Two extra conversions, two hidden margins. Look up WorldFirst. We actually hold Chinese currency.

Quick maths on where your Chinese currency wire can secretly turn into US dollars. Three points. One, the sending bank. They convert your Chinese currency instruction to US dollars because they don't actually hold Chinese currency. Margin half a percent to one percent. Two, the middle bank. They only handle US dollars on the chain. If a conversion is needed they add another point three to point eight percent. Three, the supplier's bank. They receive US dollars because no Chinese currency direct partner exists in the chain. They convert back to Chinese currency at another point three to point eight percent. Each stop adds a hidden cost. On a twenty thousand dollar wire that's two hundred and forty to five hundred dollars in conversions you never agreed to. Search WorldFirst. We hold the currency directly.

You typed Chinese currency into the wire form. Your supplier got US dollars. You both assume something went wrong. Nothing went wrong. The system worked exactly as designed. Your bank doesn't actually hold Chinese currency in its treasury. They hold US dollars. So they converted. The middle bank doesn't hold Chinese currency either. They hold US dollars. So they kept it in US dollars. Your supplier's bank received US dollars and had to convert back to Chinese currency on their end. Three banks, three chances to take a slice of the exchange rate, all hidden inside what looked like a Chinese currency wire. The fix isn't asking the bank nicely. The fix is using an account that actually holds Chinese currency. Look up WorldFirst. We do this differently.
